learning_outcomes.description: Develop a broad understanding of contemporary Korean society through the social, cultural and political issues represented in contemporary films.
Identify relevant sources and use them in developing original interpretation of the topic.
Critically analyse multi-modal resources in the social and historical contexts
Understand how South Korean media have served as a cultural medium to reflect ideologies in society and apply their knowledge in the critical review in the forms of writing and speaking.

Each week's reflection will be due at 5 pm WEDNESDAY (the day before Thursday class), WEEK 4 to WEEK 12.

WEEK 3's reflection will be due at 5 Tuesday, 11 August as Wednesday of that week is a public holiday.
assessments.category: Essay/ Critique, Participation/ Student contribution, Reflection
assessments.task_description:
Students are expected to study each week's topic by attending the Monday lecture, reading from the course reading list and writing a reflection before coming to Thursday class in which class discussion will be made based on the students' reflections. In the Weekly Reflection, the media content or online discourse surrounding the content needs to be reviewed.

Students should insert a link to the resource or add a copy of the resource on the course Padlet and write a minimum150-word reflection about the resource. The reflection must (1) summarise what the resource is about, and (2) explain how it is relevant to the topic. Students may extend their reflection to the application of such a topic in their culture or personal experiences; e.g. 'this may be compared to that of my culture, because...'.

The link to the course Padlet will be provided in the course's Blackboard. Each week's reflection will be due at 5 pm Wednesday, the day before Thursday class, every week between Week 3 and Week 12.

Weekly Reflection does not require students to read further references but asks students to explore various discourses in media around given topics and critically think about different approaches and interpretations made onto the same topic. This serves as an important foundation for tutorial discussions and will also be a key resource when completing the in-class written response each week.

Any assignment suspected for being copied from unauthorised online sources or other students' work will be referred to the Integrity Officer.

AI and MT STATEMENT

This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of the assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission, independent of AI and MT tools.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Submissions should be made through the course Padlet for each week's topic.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
This assessment encourages students to engage personally and thoughtfully with the week's assigned film, drama, or text.

During class, students will respond in writing to two questions: one asking them to reflect on the moment or aspect of the assigned material they found most striking or significant in relation to the week's theme, and one asking them to connect that reflection to the media source they explored in their Weekly Reflection.

Responses do not need to be a formal critique or draw on expert knowledge. Students are encouraged to simply share their own observations and make meaningful connections between the assigned material, the week's topic, and their independently sourced media.

Responses will be brief (approximately 200 words) and completed on paper during class time.

This assessment runs in the tutorials from Week 3 to Week 13, and the best 6 submissions out of all completed responses will be counted toward the final mark. Students are encouraged to complete this written response every week, as regular reflection throughout the semester will deepen their engagement with each topic and ultimately support the development of their final essay.

In Week 2, students will complete a practice written response to help them learn how to plan, organise, and compose their work before the assessed written responses begin in Week 3.

AI and MT STATEMENT

This assessment task is to be completed in person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: This assessment will be conducted in person in class.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description:
For the Final Essay assignment, students will be asked to choose one question from a list of several essay prompts and write a 2,000-word (+/-10%) essay. Possible topics for the essay will be discussed in class, and the Final Essay topics will be released in Week 13 on Blackboard. Students should submit their essay via Turnitin by the due date.

Essay Requirements:

Students will write a critical analysis essay on social and/or cultural phenomena of Korea. Your essay must effectively utilise primary course resources (films, TV dramas or books), class discussions, and relevant social media discourse related to your chosen topic. You are expected to demonstrate engagement with course content by incorporating lecture materials, course readings, and insights from class discussions into your analysis.

To develop your argument, draw from weekly reflections, written responses, and in-class discussions covered throughout the semester. Your essay should manifest your understanding of class discussions by effectively referencing sources while offering your own thoughtful interpretations and critical reflections.

Source Requirements:

  • A minimum of three (3) references from the course reading list must be cited in the essay.
  • Integration of relevant lecture materials and class discussion points is mandatory.
  • Students are encouraged to reference media content or cultural phenomena discussed in class to support their arguments.

Citation and Formatting: Clear citations of all sources (including social media content) using APA referencing style are essential. This includes proper in-text citations and a complete reference list. You are not expected to cite lecture slides.

AI and MT STATEMENT

This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A coversheet acknowledging the use of AI will be mandatory to be attached. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Submit your Final Essay via Turnitin on the course Blackboard.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
Students will submit a 5-minute video reflection alongside their essay. In the video, students will briefly outline the key argument of their essay (3-4 minutes) and reflect on how their engagement with Korean films, dramas, and other media throughout the course has shaped their understanding of Korean society and culture (1-2 minutes). Students are encouraged to draw on specific moments from course materials, class discussions, or their own weekly reflections that were particularly meaningful to their thinking.

Before beginning the presentation, students must hold their student ID next to their face in front of the camera to verify their identity. The video should be uploaded together with the essay by the due date.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Video Reflections will be submitted through Assignment in Blackboard.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

Introduction to course

Lecture: Overview of the course

Tutorial: Screening of "Ode to my father"; Discussion on the familism and modern history of South Korea; Critical review of social media discourse on the topic

Learning outcomes: L02, L04

learning_activities.topic:
Familism in "Ode to My Father" and "Mother"

Lecture: Discussion on familism

Tutorial: Social discourse about 'mother' and 'father' in Korean literature, TV dramas and films; Critical review of social media discourse on the topic


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

learning_activities.topic:
Multiculturalism in "Extraordinary Attorney Woo" and "Punch"

Lecture: Discussion on the multiculturalism in Korea and outside Korea

Tutorial: "Itaewon Class", "Minari", "Punch". "Pachinko" and "Extraordinary Attorney Woo" in the context of multiculturalism; Critical review of social media discourse on the topic

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Social inequality in "Parasite" and "Squid Game"

Lecture: Discussion on social inequality in contemporary Korea

Tutorial: The young Koreans' life in "Parasite", "Squid Game" and "The Glory"; Critical review of social media discourse on the topic

Food and culture in "Crying in H Mart" and "Little Forest"

Lecture: Discussion on Korean food being part of the Korean Wave as well as a popular theme in Korean media

Tutorial: Koreans identified by Korean food in "Crying in H Mart", "Littel Forest" and "Youn's Kitchen"; Critical review of social media discourse on the topic
